title: Exact formulas for random growth off a flat interface
speaker: Daniel Remenik
abstract: We will describe formulas for the asymmetric simple exclusion process (ASEP) starting from half-flat and flat initial data. The formulas are for the exponential moments of the height function associated with ASEP. They lead to explicit formulas for certain generating functions of the model, which in the flat case can be expressed as a Fredholm Pfaffian. We will explain also how these formulas can be used to provide formal derivations of the conjectured limiting fluctuations, and discuss some related models.

proof_stages.label: ASEP height growth and TASEP benchmarks
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines one-dimensional ASEP, its height function, and the step/flat/stationary initial geometries, then recalls that TASEP has determinantal formulas leading to GUE, GOE, and Airy-process limits.

proof_stages.label: Obstruction beyond determinantal and replica settings
proof_stages.summary: For partially asymmetric ASEP the determinantal structure disappears: step and half-stationary data have heavily processed exact formulas, flat data resist asymptotics, and KPZ/delta-Bose-gas replica heuristics suggest but do not justify a moment-summation approach.

proof_stages.label: ASEP moment boundary-value problem
proof_stages.summary: The key observable built from tau-powers of particle counts encodes the height function and is characterized as the unique solution of free evolution equations, exclusion boundary conditions, and an initial condition.

proof_stages.label: Half-flat contour ansatz and cancellations
proof_stages.summary: A Bethe-ansatz-style contour integral with double products, exponential time factors, and specially placed poles is checked against the moment equations, with the alternating half-flat initial condition working only because extra residues cancel.

proof_stages.label: Tau-deformed summation and half-flat asymptotics
proof_stages.summary: The moment formula is converted into a form suitable for summation, a tau-deformed exponential/Mellin-Barnes trick gives a distribution-determining formula, and formal critical point analysis yields the Airy 2-to-1 half-flat asymptotics despite unresolved double-product bounds.

proof_stages.label: Flat limit and Fredholm Pfaffian structure
proof_stages.summary: By sending the observation point far into the flat side of the half-flat formula, the speaker obtains flat ASEP moment formulas with Pfaffians and then a Fredholm Pfaffian generating formula, formally recovering GOE asymptotics while noting remaining moment-problem difficulties.

examples.name: TASEP/last-passage percolation benchmark
examples.note: The totally asymmetric case p=0, q=1 is used as a familiar exactly solvable comparison where determinantal formulas give GUE/GOE and Airy-process limits, highlighting what is lost for partially asymmetric ASEP.
examples.engagement_reason: The TASEP/last-passage percolation case is used as a known benchmark with stated fluctuation limits and determinantal formulas, but the speaker does not derive or compute them.

passes.reason: TASEP/LPP is the one-way p=0/q=1 boundary, zero-temperature solvable limit of ASEP, used as a benchmark rather than a refutation.

examples.name: Airy linearization of divergent replica moments
examples.note: The Airy-transform identity linearizing an e^{n^3} growth factor is presented as the formal trick behind replica summation and motivates the later microscopic tau-deformed summation where the moments are initially bounded.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker explicitly manipulates the divergent replica moment series by using the Airy transform identity to linearize the e^{n^3} growth and explains how this enables a formal summation.

passes.reason: The Airy linearization is an ordinary special-function trick, presented in the setting where divergent replica moments obstruct the naive moment-generating-function argument.
The Airy transform trick is presented in the context of divergent replica moments as an obstruction to a rigorous moment-generating-function argument.

examples.name: Q-TASEP/Q-Whittaker contour formulas
examples.note: The contour integral formulas from Q-TASEP and Macdonald-process theory are described as an analogy that helped suggest the ASEP ansatz, even though ASEP itself is not part of that hierarchy.
examples.engagement_reason: Q-TASEP/Q-Whittaker contour formulas are cited as analogous prior formulas and limiting inputs, but the talk does not concretely run or derive them.

examples.name: Period-three flat initial condition
examples.note: An audience question about putting one particle every three sites is used to illustrate that the pole-cancellation method is highly special to the alternating flat data and does not presently cover all macroscopically flat profiles.
examples.engagement_reason: The period-three flat initial condition is raised only as an unsolved variant expected to have GOE behavior, with no calculation performed on it.

passes.reason: The period-three initial condition is a highly regular periodic flat configuration used to show that the exact half-flat method does not extend to all flat data.
